Cheetah African Wild Dog
🦛
Africa

Hippopotamus

Hippopotamus amphibius
Status Vulnerable
Habitat Rivers, Lakes, Wetlands
Diet Herbivore
Lifespan 40-50 years
Weight 1,300-1,500 kg (male), 1,300-1,400 kg (female)
×

The hippopotamus (Hippopotamus amphibius) stands as one of Africa's most formidable and misunderstood animals. Despite their seemingly docile, almost comical appearance, hippos rank among the continent's most dangerous mammals, responsible for an estimated 500 human deaths annually. These massive semi-aquatic herbivores, second only to elephants in land mammal size, spend their days submerged in rivers and lakes to protect their sensitive skin from the harsh African sun. At night, they emerge to graze on land, consuming up to 40 kg of grass. Their territorial nature, combined with surprising speed on land (up to 30 km/h) and water, makes them unpredictable and potentially deadly.

Habitat & Range

Hippos inhabit rivers, lakes, swamps, and wetlands throughout sub-Saharan Africa where permanent water sources exist. In East Africa, major populations occur in the Mara River, Lake Victoria, Lake Tanganyika, and numerous smaller water bodies. They require water deep enough to submerge completely (at least 1.5 meters) and nearby grasslands for nighttime grazing. Their range extends up to 10 km from water sources depending on food availability. During droughts, they may be forced to congregate in remaining water sources, leading to increased aggression and conflict.

Behavior & Social Structure

Hippos live in social groups called pods or bloats, typically consisting of 10-30 individuals dominated by a single territorial male. These groups establish specific territories in stretches of water, which males aggressively defend against rivals. During the day, hippos remain almost entirely submerged, surfacing every 3-5 minutes to breathe while sleeping. At dusk, they emerge to graze on land, following established paths to feeding areas. They are surprisingly vocal animals, communicating through honks, grunts, and wheezes that carry across water surfaces. Despite their bulk, they are graceful swimmers and can walk along river bottoms.

Diet & Nutrition

As grazers, hippos feed exclusively on grasses and aquatic vegetation, consuming approximately 40 kg of plant matter nightly. Despite their semi-aquatic lifestyle, they rarely feed in water, preferring to graze on land during nighttime foraging expeditions. Their specialized digestive system, featuring a three-chambered stomach, efficiently processes coarse grasses. They typically graze within 10 km of their daytime water refuge, returning to the same spots each morning. This grazing behavior significantly impacts riverbank vegetation and creates pathways used by other animals.

Key Facts

  • Third largest land mammal after elephants and rhinos
  • Responsible for approximately 500 human deaths annually in Africa
  • Can run at 30 km/h on land despite bulky appearance
  • Spend up to 16 hours per day submerged in water
  • Secrete natural sunscreen (blood sweat) that turns red in sunlight
  • Can hold breath underwater for up to 5 minutes

Conservation Status

Classified as Vulnerable by the IUCN Red List. Hippopotamus populations have declined by 20% over the past two decades, with fewer than 130,000 individuals remaining in the wild. Once abundant across sub-Saharan Africa, they have disappeared from much of their historic range due to habitat loss, human-wildlife conflict, and illegal hunting for meat and ivory (canine teeth). East African strongholds include the Serengeti-Mara ecosystem, Ruaha National Park, and the Kafue Flats. Conservation efforts focus on protecting wetland habitats and mitigating human-hippo conflict in agricultural areas.

Primary Threats

  • Habitat loss and degradation of wetlands
  • Human-wildlife conflict in agricultural areas
  • Illegal hunting for meat and ivory (canine teeth)
  • Drought and climate change affecting water sources
  • Water pollution and development of waterways

Related Species

🐊 Nile Crocodile 🐘 African Elephant 🐃 African Buffalo

Frequently Asked Questions

How big is an Hippopotamus?

Hippopotamuss typically weigh 1,300-1,500 kg (male), 1,300-1,400 kg (female) and can be identified by their distinctive features. They are native to Rivers, Lakes, Wetlands.

Are Hippopotamuss endangered?

The Hippopotamus is currently classified as Vulnerable by the IUCN Red List. Classified as Vulnerable by the IUCN Red List. Hippopotamus populations have declined by 20% over the past two decades, with fewer than 130,000 indivi...

What do Hippopotamuss eat?

Hippopotamuss are herbivore. As grazers, hippos feed exclusively on grasses and aquatic vegetation, consuming approximately 40 kg of plant matter nig...

How long do Hippopotamuss live?

In the wild, Hippopotamuss typically live for 40-50 years. Their lifespan can vary based on environmental conditions and conservation efforts.
